As for the BoJ, policy 'normalization', which entails a transition from negative to zero and, eventually, positive interest rates, has begun. So far, though, the pace of 'normalization' has been very gradual. Note that since the BoJ reversed course in March and hiked another 25bps in July, financial market volatility has kept policymakers cautious ("we can afford to spend some time in making a policy decision").

Hence, the swaps market has taken its cues and, accordingly, priced in policy rates of up to 0.4% through the March 2025 meeting and approximately 0.5% through September next year. In line with this cautious view of future interest rate hikes, SMFG has yet to fully regain its July highs and still trades at a de-rated book multiple. In sum, a low bar with ample room to re-rate once interest rate expectations reverse after this month's general election.

P&L Momentum Keeps the Setup Interesting…

In the meantime, company-level data out of SMFG has been positive heading into next month's half-year report. Per commentary from this year's IR day, for instance, early fiscal Q2 is already benefiting from the same momentum seen in Q1, which saw the bank tracking well ahead of target on both underlying profits and return on equity.

External data is also tracking a lot better than the bank's full-year guidance assumptions, most notably a 140 USD/JPY exchange rate (vs. 150 currently) and a 0.1% policy rate (i.e., no additional rate hikes). The latter is key, as it allows SMFG, along with the other Japanese megabanks, to pass through higher short-term prime rates to variable-rate mortgages and loans. Recent news flow suggests this process has already begun sector-wide, so expect to see a boost to SMFG's reported interest income next month (each 10bps rate increase adds ~JPY40bn of interest income per FQ1 sensitivities).

In sum, even though management says they aren't being as conservative with guidance this time around ("based on the most like scenario as of this May, while targets of last year were based on more conservative assumptions"), there's ample room for SMFG to outperform its guidance bar.

…while Balance Sheet Adds Upside Levers

On the other side of the equation is SMFG's improving balance sheet. Most notably, its 'stickier' deposit base, which is, to some extent, thanks to the healthy competitive dynamics in Japan, where the focus has been on differentiation, rather than deposit rate competition.

In SMFG's case, its efforts to increase deposit 'stickiness' is centered on "Olive", a 'one-stop shop' spanning banking, payments, insurance, and brokerage services for retail customers. Per IR day disclosures, "Olive" traction has only gone from strength to strength, with yen deposits from new account openers accelerating to +135% YoY and new account openings well on track to hit management's 12m mid-term target.

The rest of SMFG's balance sheet offers investors ways to win as well. From its 15% stake in high-flying US investment bank Jefferies (JEF) (see Jefferies: A Warren Buffett Stock With Runway) to the unwinding of its cross-shareholdings, SMFG's capital allocation is clearly moving in the right direction.

Also worth noting is the bank's best-in-class capital ratio, which, as of Q2, stood at 10.3% on a Basel III finalized basis (vs. 10% target). In other words, there's capital available for more buyback announcements, if not at next month's earnings report, then later over the course of the year. Alternatively, higher dividends (vs. the current ~3% yield) may also be on the cards, particularly with the bank tracking well ahead of its targeted run rate.

Summing Up

SMFG, along with the rest of Japan's megabank stocks, haven't quite recovered since the volatility of Q3. As things stabilize internally and externally through year-end, however, the bank has a clear re-rating path from here. In the meantime, not a lot of SMFG's upside levers, either on the P&L side or the balance sheet, are adequately accounted for in management's (unchanged) mid-term targets, nor in the stock's stubborn discount to book. Hence, a great risk/reward for investors here while getting paid a ~3% yield to wait.

data.title: Lululemon Athletica: A Premium Brand Trading At A Discount
data.description:
I've always enjoyed working out and until recently, I didn't pay too much attention to the clothes I wore, to me, it was all about getting the exercise done. However, a few months ago, I got a pair of sweatpants from a company I had not heard a lot from, but they were some of the most comfortable pants I've ever worn. Stretchy and comfortable pants can make your workouts more enjoyable.

The company that produced the sweatpants was Lululemon Athletica Inc (NASDAQ:LULU). My friend, who got me the pants, was an ambassador for the brand, and ever since I've been keeping an eye on the company. Still, while I liked its products, I didn't like the price it was trading at. But lately, the high price and expectations the company had, have drastically come down (almost 47% from its previous high) and now present an attractive opportunity for many investors.

The Company

Lululemon was founded almost 25 years ago as a brand that targeted a specific niche, mainly people who enjoyed doing yoga and were looking for stretchy and comfy pants, which were not that common at the time. Since then, the company has expanded and now manufactures sports-related clothes as well as fashion clothes dedicated to the "Athleisure" style.

What separates the company from its competitors is the way it promotes and sells its products, rather than partnering with well-known athletes, Lululemon focuses on finding local brand ambassadors and creating experiences inside its stores, where from time to time you can expect to be welcomed by a friendly staff into a fitness class given by a local instructor. Has this strategy worked? The numbers speak for themselves as sales over the last 10 years have grown at a 19.54% CAGR and even its EBIT has grown at a similar pace. To put it into context in the last 10 years Nike's (NYSE:NKE) revenues have grown at a rate of 5.67%, nonetheless, it is important to consider that Nike is a more mature company than Lululemon.

While the company's revenues do look impressive what sets the company apart from its competitors is the high EBIT margin it has, even against the market leader (Nike) they have a superior advantage.

How is the company able to achieve such margins?

The first reason why Lululemon enjoys high margins is its ability to charge higher prices for its products as consumers value the quality and innovation they offer. As previously mentioned, the company tries to create more of a culture and positive experience around its brand, rather than just making it a mere transaction-based interaction when you interact with it.

Lululemon's high operating margin is also a product of its high gross margin, as it employs efficient inventory management that allows it to forecast demand and avoid inventory write-offs. Also, they are constantly looking for opportunities to improve margins, for instance, in 2023 gross margins increased by 290bp due to efficiencies in freight costs, rates went down and there was a shift away from air freight.

The company also enjoys a lean-vertical structure, selling mainly through two channels, online and in company-operated stores. This structure allows it to bypass the higher markups that wholesalers charge and have better control over the experience customers have. To put into perspective the benefits of selling online rather than relying on stores, we can take a look at the percentage of sales companies derive from e-commerce:

  • Lululemon: 45%
  • Nike: 26%
  • Adidas: 20%

In summary, Lululemon has been able to differentiate its business model from its competitors by targeting a niche in the market that values quality and comfort, as well as having a marketing program directed towards finding ambassadors and operating a light-cost structure focused on a direct-to-consumer approach. All of this has allowed the company to have superior margins and profitability in comparison to its competitors and even against its history, as can be seen in Seeking Alpha's profitability score of A.

The Industry

The current sports apparel market is worth around 211.57 billion and has a projected CAGR of 4.68% for the next 8 years. Thus, we are talking about a mature industry with growth projections just slightly above the World's GDP growth projections.

The industry, as acknowledged by the company, is highly competitive, doing Porter's 5 forces analysis we get the following results:

  • Competitive Rivals: High - The industry is highly competitive, with many businesses fighting for market share.
  • Entry Barriers: Low - New companies can enter the space with little capital, just look at new competitors attracting market share such as Alo Yoga and Gymshark.
  • Suppliers Power: Medium - While many companies have developed strong manufacturing processes with suppliers, the switching cost to a new supplier is relatively low and with emerging markets competing with low labor more switching could be expected in the future.
  • Consumer Power: High - Given the ample choices customers have due to the low switching costs in the industry, they can pressure companies to offer better pricing through discounts.
  • Threat of Substitutes: High - Many clothing brands, such as fast-fashion ones, offer sporty clothes at more affordable prices, especially for customers who are just looking for clothes that look good and provide the "Athleisure" style at half the price of traditional sports companies.

After the previous analysis, we can conclude that the industry is a highly competitive one and the enterprises that manage to get most of the market share need to be perceived by consumers as offering value through their recognized brands, providing companies with a moat, albeit a very narrow one.

Lululemon given the high margins compared to competitors, does indeed show signs of having a moat based on offering premium and quality products that allows it to charge higher prices, nonetheless, the industry's structure makes it difficult for it to be a wide moat.

The Value

Having analyzed the company and the business model, it is time to determine whether the price is trading below fair value.

Revenues

Normally, I would assume a growth rate that is close to the company's historical trend, however, given that we are dealing with a young growth company, I will instead focus on the total addressable market and the share Lululemon could achieve.

As mentioned previously, as of 2024, the size of the industry is $211.57 billion. Taking into account Lululemon's LTM revenues, this would give the company a market share of 4.7%. For my valuation, I assume it can reach a market share of 7.5%, which would entail the company significantly reducing the double-digit growth it achieved in the last decade. This reduction, to me, seems reasonable, as Lululemon is becoming a more mature company and, to keep having the strong competitive advantage, it needs to keep marketing itself as a premium brand, which limits the potential size it can reach.

For some, this slowing growth might be too low - and, perhaps, I agree - but I rather be on the conservative side. The company currently has a plan known as the Power of Three ×2 growth, aiming to double its men's business, double its digital business, and quadruple its international business by 2026. This strategy coupled with its growing international expansion (specifically in the EMEA and APAC regions) does make at least a single-digit growth possible.

Margins

I already discussed a lot about Lululemon's best-in-class EBIT margin and while I do expect them to keep having strong margins, I'd rather again be cautious and expect them to settle at levels close to 20%, a reduction of almost 15% from the LTM number. I mainly assume this because the industry is highly competitive, and sooner or later for the company to keep growing and staying competitive, some margin level would need to be sacrificed.

Tax rate and WACC

For the tax rate, I assume it reaches a global marginal rate of 25%.

For its cost of capital, I use a regression beta of 1.25, an implied ERP of 3.94%, a risk-free rate of 4.09%, and a spread of 0.60% over the risk-free rate for the little debt the company has. Putting all of these numbers into the WACC formula, I get a cost of capital of 8.80%. However, as the company approaches its terminal value I do expect it to be a more mature and stable business with more debt in its structure, giving them a lower cost of capital of 8%.

Reinvestment

The company has historically had on average a sales-to-capital ratio of 3.50x, but currently, they have expansion targets that would warrant more investments, so for the first five years I assume the ratio is 1.50x and ultimately settle for the remainder of the forecast period at 3.50x.

ROIC

The ROIC I get for the 10 years of my valuation is the result of the previous inputs, yielding an average number of 30%. However, after year 10 I forecast that it will drop to 15%, giving the company some competitive advantage still, but that will be reduced as more competitors enter the industry.

All the previous inputs are summarized in the image below. At a price of $283.71, the company seems to be undervalued by close to 24%, thus yielding an attractive entry point.

Risks

Competition Risk

The company operates in a very competitive industry where the only way to get a return above its cost of capital is by creating a strong brand, but in an industry with low barriers of entry, the advantage can quickly disappear.

Economic Risk

Sportswear clothing is a discretionary buy that can be severely impacted by an economic slowdown.

Brand Reputation

The company relies on its local ambassadors to promote its products, however, if some of them were to engage in questionable activities the brand could lose its appeal to customers.

Raising Costs

Inflationary pressures and raising wages could negatively affect margins.

Summary

Lululemon has been able to distance itself from its competitors by having a different marketing strategy, direct-to-consumer sales, efficient inventory management and overall focusing on delivering quality on its products that pushes customers to pay a premium price. The company might have had ambitious expectations that were reflected in its price, but now, those expectations might have come down more than warranted and investors should consider adding exposure at current levels.

data.title: Rates Spark: Markets Now Contemplating A 50bp December ECB Cut
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker and Benjamin Schroeder

ECB cut by 25bp and leaving dovish aftertaste

The European Central Bank cut the deposit rate by 25bp to 3.25% as expected, but what we did not expect was the overly dovish angle in the communication surrounding the decision. During the Q&A, ECB President Christine Lagarde highlighted that the decision was unanimous, which means that the earlier pushback by hawks did not materialise in an opposing vote. On the other hand, growth concerns seemed to be downplayed, yet this did not persuade markets into a more hawkish perception. Risks stemming from a Trump victory were also referenced, giving market doves another hook for reinforcing expectations for a December cut.

In fact, and to our surprise, markets now price in about a 25% chance of a 50bp cut in December. Markets have got ahead of themselves before, and based on the current economic data, we think the positioning is overly ambitious. Having said that, the catalysts to persuade rates markets away from a 50bp cut are hard to see as economic data is likely to continue softening for the time being. Add further downside inflation surprises, and markets may well see an ECB tempted to accelerate the trajectory back to neutral.

Now that traders are seriously contemplating 50bp cuts, the EUR curve could see further steepening from the front-end. Until we have clarity about Harris vs. Trump, more easing on the back of growth concerns will be the prevailing theme. The endpoint for the ECB seems to be relatively anchored for now, and thus, we’re talking about an acceleration of cuts rather than the number of cuts. The back-end of the curve remains more driven by global dynamics, reflected in the strong spillovers from the bear steepening of the UST curve on better-than-expected economic data.

Today’s events and market view

The ECB will release the survey of professional forecasters, but not much other data of relevance features on the calendar. We will watch out for the usual post-meeting flurry of commentary from ECB members, especially since the hawks appear to have fallen in line with Thursday’s decision. No speakers are officially scheduled, though.

The US will release housing start numbers, and a few Fed speakers, including Raphael Bostic, Christopher Waller and Neel Kashkari, fill the calendar for the day.

More attention will be paid to sovereign ratings over coming weeks. This Friday evening, the focus is on Italy with both S&P and Fitch reviewing their BBB ratings, both currently with stable outlooks. Sentiment appears to be geared towards the upside, underscoring an ongoing reordering within the semi-core and periphery eurozone sovereign bond space.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

data.title: AstroNova's Pricing Is Optimistic And Not An Opportunity
data.description:
AstroNova (NASDAQ:ALOT) is a printer manufacturer with a segment dedicated to labels (from small businesses to industrial use) and another one dedicated to specialized printers used inside planes.

The label printer business is competitive and cyclical and has a lower margin than the aircraft printer business. However, the former has the biggest capacity to grow long-term, compared to the latter, which is predicated on aircraft building recovery and then becoming a mature/declining business.

AstroNova's financial and capital allocation have been good over time, with maybe some excessive M&A activity this year, leading to more leverage than would be desirable, although still very manageable.

The name trades at a forward P/E ratio of about 18x. This multiple makes sense if the company can deliver operationally levered growth. However, if it can't (and both history and current business conditions do not help with this respect), then growing into the multiple will take a long time, and the price does not compensate for other risks (mainly recessionary in the label business). For that reason, I believe AstroNova is a Hold at these prices.

Company intro

Label printer segment: AstroNova's Product Identification business specializes in label printers. These range from small domestic-use printers (used by entrepreneurs and small businesses) sold under the brand Quick Label to much larger industrial-setting printers sold under the brand Trojan Label. Recently, the company acquired a company specializing in logistics and postal printing (Astro Machine, 2022) and another one specializing in packaging (MTEX NS, 2024). We will comment on these acquisitions later.

The segment generates about 2/3 of the company's revenues but only half of its profitability because it has a lower margin than the aircraft business. The machines do not seem particularly complex, and AstroNova is not a big player ($100 million revenues yearly in a market worth several dozen billion, at least).

The segment has a low margin (10/12% before overhead) and probably suffers from investment-driven cycles (like most machinery). On the other hand, it has a resilient characteristic driven by the consumption of supplies: after a printer sale is made, a recurrent stream of supplies will probably follow. Supplies make up about 30% of the company's revenue.

Aircraft printer segment: AstroNova's second segment is called Test & Measurement because the company started in the test-printer niche. However, that business' size today is undisclosed but probably inconsequential.

The T&M segment actually gets its business from selling aircraft printers. Here, the company is dominant (with contracts with Airbus, Boeing, Embraer, Gulf Stream, and the military) and enjoys higher operating margins (around 20/25%). The business is more stable (depends on aircraft construction and deliveries), but is also smaller in size. The number of planes built grows over time, but given that AstroNova already dominates, it doesn't have much to win above the market.

Historically low and cyclical margins: If we look at the company's public financial history, we can observe that they have been cyclical and that they have been relatively low (with an average of 4% on the operating side).

Ok capital allocation: AstroNova has not needed much CAPEX historically, ranging from 1/2% of revenues. The company has made several intelligent acquisitions so far. For example, the company acquired most of its competitors in the aircraft printer business, leading to a dominant position. Then, in 2022, it acquired Astro Machine for about $6 million (net of tangible assets acquired), and the business had generated almost $4 million in net income by the end of 2023.

Maybe more questionable is the company's latest acquisition of MTEX NS, for about $19 million, including earn outs and net of tangible assets, for a company that generated only EUR 3 million in EBITDA in FY22.

Leveraged but manageable: One of the questionable points of the MTEX NS acquisition is the increase in the company's debt, which pays a high interest of about SOFR (or EURIBOR) + 2%. This debt does not represent a large problem (its interest is about $3 million per year versus $11/12 million in operating income) but will take time to be repaid from net income.

Valuation

Business opportunity in labels: The labels segment probably has the best opportunity to grow long-term, based on the gigantic size of the broad label printing market versus on-plane printing. For example, AstroNova can grow into adjacent markets in label printing but not in on-plane printing.

Of course, the business is also heavily exposed to the business cycle. For example, in 2023, the label segment was flat vs 2022, despite the acquisition of Astro Machine in 2022 (FY23), which added $18 million in sales. This means that during 2023, the organic business shrunk by almost 20% and probably remains challenged.

Business opportunity aircraft: The aircraft printer business has a lower chance of growing fast over the long term simply because it needs the market to expand because it has occupied the whole category, whereas the label business can get share.

In the short term, many AstroNova bulls believe that the business could expand as much as 30% if global airplane deliveries (around 1,200 in 2023) reached pre-pandemic levels (about 1,600 in 2018). This is uncertain, given the gap is in a big part explained by supply chain disruptions and challenges at the second-largest builder, Boeing.

Operating leverage: Operating leverage is an essential driver of future profitability. Over time, AstroNova has shown cyclical but not particularly leverageable margins.

In the post-pandemic era, margins have expanded (see chart below, which includes some non-recurring factors in OpEx). However, the aircraft business recovered a lot in 2022/23 driving aggregate margins up. In the future, the label business has a higher chance of growing, and it will probably drive the company's aggregate margin down.

Current yields and expectations: The company is guiding revenues to grow MSD organically this year and for MTEX NS to deliver between $8/10 million in revenues. That would put yearly revenues for FY25 (calendar 2024) in the realm of $165 million. Applying EBITDA margins of 8% (the company guides for one or two points above, but I am not adding back SBC) and considering 1% of revenues in CAPEX, we could be talking of a cash-based operating income of $11.5 million. Removing $3.4 million in interest ($45 million by 7.5% interest) leads to about $8 million in pre-tax income, which after 25% taxes leads to about $6 million in net income.

Compared to this, the company's market cap is $107 million, meaning the company trades at an expected 18x P/E, or a low yield of only 5.6%, barely above Treasuries. Of course, the name trades at that valuation because shareholders expect growth. How much exactly depends a lot on operating leverage.

For example, with no operating leverage, for the company to deliver (what I would consider the minimum of) 12% yield, it should grow revenues to almost $300 million, or almost double what it is expected to generate in FY24 (this comes from applying a 7% post-CAPEX margin to $13 million in net income plus 25% taxes and $3.4 million in interest). This seems extremely optimistic. Even at a 10% growth rate, it would take 7 years.

On the other hand, with fixed OpEx (and assuming current gross margins of 37%), the company only needs to grow revenues 7% to deliver that 12% yield ($177 million in revenues with 37% gross margins and $45 million in OpEx). This does not sound so optimistic, but it actually is. It implies an aggregate operating margin of almost 12% compared to the current 7%. This seems unlikely, given the label business margins do not reach 12% even during good years.

Conclusion

AstroNova is an interesting business, but I believe it deserves to generate a sufficient return of about 12% before considering it fairly valued. The reason is that the name is still a microcap with low and cyclical margins, deriving most income and future growth from a competitive industry in which it is tiny.

The stock is far from providing that return today, probably because there is optimism about its future growth. Although that optimism might be somewhat justified, it seems exaggerated, as evidenced by the estimations above. The company should leverage its costs above management guidance, or grow its revenues 100% before generating a sufficient return.

If positive developments are already embedded in the stock price, then there are not a lot of fundamental upsides, unless one expects the market to become incrementally optimistic (playing a bigger fool game). I prefer situations where optimism is not priced in and where conservatism or even pessimism prevails. For that reason, I believe ALOT is not an opportunity at these prices, and rate the name as Hold.
